content:
First, label the $3\times3$ squares in a checkerboard fashion: there are exactly 5 “black’’ squares (positions with $(i+j)\bmod2=0$) and 4 “white’’ squares.  Any edge–adjacency constraint forces the board to be bipartite in parity: one color must carry all the odds, the other all the evens.  Since there are 5 odd numbers $\{1,3,5,7,9\}$ and only 4 evens, the odds must go on the 5 black squares and the 4 evens on the 4 white squares.  

Hence the total number of valid fillings (ignoring symmetries) is
\[
\underbrace{5!}_{\text{arrange the 5 odds on the black squares}}
\;\times\;
\underbrace{4!}_{\text{arrange the 4 evens on the white squares}}
\;=\;120\cdot24\;=\;2880.
\]

We only consider rotational equivalence (the cyclic group $C_4$ of order 4).  By Burnside’s Lemma, the number of orbits is
\[
\frac1{4}\Bigl(\#\text{fixed by }0^\circ+\#\text{fixed by }90^\circ
+\#\text{fixed by }180^\circ+\#\text{fixed by }270^\circ\Bigr).
\]
The identity ($0^\circ$) fixes all $2880$.  A nontrivial rotation (either $90^\circ,180^\circ,270^\circ$) would force some distinct squares to carry the same label, which is impossible under our distinct‐numbers‐from‐1–9 hypothesis.  Hence none of the nontrivial rotations fixes any arrangement.  Therefore
\[
\#\text{orbits}
=\frac{1}{4}\,(2880+0+0+0)
=720.
\]

Answer: \(\boxed{720}.\)
